title: Should you charge your EV to 100%?
description: Charging your EV to 100% every day can shorten battery life. When 80% is the right daily limit, when a full charge is fine, and what each charge costs.
blurb: Why most EVs are happier at 80%, when a full charge is fine, and what it costs either way.
lede: Most electric cars have a charge limit setting buried in a menu, and plenty of owners never touch it. Whether you should is one of the few EV questions with a genuinely useful answer, because the way lithium batteries age means the difference between an 80% habit and a 100% habit can show up in your range figure years down the road. The right answer for your car takes about two minutes to work out, it depends almost entirely on which battery chemistry you have, and setting it up costs nothing.
short_answer: For most EVs, no, not every day. If your car has an NMC or NCA battery (most long-range models), set a daily charge limit of around 80% and save 100% for the night before a long trip. If it has an LFP battery (common in standard-range models), charging to 100% is fine and most manufacturers ask you to do it at least once a week. What genuinely harms any battery is sitting parked at 100% for days, not the occasional full charge you drive off on.

sections.html:
A lithium battery cell is under the most electrical stress at the very top of its charge window. As the state of charge climbs towards full, the cell voltage rises, and high voltage held over time is one of the main drivers of what engineers call calendar ageing: the slow, permanent loss of capacity that happens whether you drive or not. Heat makes it worse, which is why a car left at 100% on a warm driveway for a week ages faster than the same car left at 60%.

That word held is doing the work in that sentence. Charging to 100% and setting off within a few hours is a very different thing from charging to 100% every night and letting the car sit at full all day. The first is normal use that every EV is designed for. The second keeps the cells at their highest-stress state for hundreds of hours a month, and that is the habit worth breaking.

There are two smaller effects on top. Rapid chargers slow dramatically above about 80% to protect the cells, so the last 20% on a public rapid can take as long as the previous 40%. And a battery at 100% has nowhere to put energy from regenerative braking, so the car uses its friction brakes more and feels slightly different to drive for the first few miles.

None of this means a full charge is dangerous. Real-world fleet data, such as the large Geotab study of many thousands of EVs, puts average battery degradation at roughly 2% of capacity a year, and the curve flattens after the first couple of years. Charging habits nudge that figure; they do not transform it. There is a fuller picture in our guide to how long EV batteries last.

sections.h2: Check your chemistry first: NMC or LFP
sections.html:
The single most important thing to know before picking a charge limit is which battery chemistry your car uses, because the advice is close to opposite for the two main types.

NMC and NCA (most long-range EVs)

Nickel-based chemistries are energy dense, which is why long-range models use them, but they are the ones that dislike sitting at high charge. For these cars, the standard advice from manufacturers is a daily limit of 80% or so, with 100% reserved for long trips. Some makers suggest 90% as the daily ceiling; your handbook or charging screen will say.

LFP (lithium iron phosphate)

LFP packs, fitted to many standard-range models from Tesla, MG, BYD and a growing list of others, are far more tolerant of being charged to full. More than that, their very flat voltage curve makes it hard for the car to estimate the state of charge accurately, so manufacturers typically ask you to charge to 100% regularly, often once a week, to let the battery management system recalibrate. If your car has an LFP pack and you religiously stop at 80%, the worst outcome is usually a state-of-charge gauge that drifts and occasionally surprises you.

How to find out which you have

  • Check the vehicle specification sheet or handbook; LFP is normally stated.
  • Look at the maker's charging guidance in the car's own screen or app. "Charge to 100% weekly" wording is the LFP giveaway; "set a daily limit of 80%" wording points to NMC or NCA.
  • Standard-range versions of a model are more likely to be LFP, long-range versions more likely nickel-based, but verify rather than assume.

Per kWh, the last 20% costs exactly the same as the first 20%, so this is not a case of the top of the battery being expensive energy. What changes the money picture is where you do the charging. Here is the same top-up priced four ways, for a car with a 60 kWh usable battery arriving at 20% charge.

Where you chargeRate per kWh20% to 80% (36 kWh)20% to 100% (48 kWh)
Home, EV off-peak tariff7p£2.52£3.36
Home, standard rate26.11p£9.40£12.53
Public standard charger54p£19.44£25.92
Public rapid charger79p£28.44£37.92

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026; EV off-peak example 7p; public charger averages from Zapmap, mid 2026. Figures exclude charging losses, typically 10 to 15% on a home wallbox.

Two things jump out. First, the gap between an 80% and a 100% charge at home off-peak is well under £1, so nobody should pick a charge limit to save money on electricity. Second, the place where 80% really earns its keep is the public rapid charger: that last 20% costs £9.48 at 79p per kWh and arrives at a crawl because of the charging taper, so on a road trip it is almost always faster and cheaper to unplug at 80% and stop again later. Our guides to home charging costs and EV charging times cover both halves of that trade-off in detail.

sections.h2: When charging to 100% is the right call
sections.html:
A daily limit is a default, not a rule. There are several situations where a full charge is exactly what you want.

  • The night before a long journey. Charge to 100% overnight on your cheap rate and leave in the morning. The battery spends only a few hours at full, which is harmless, and you start the trip with maximum range and the cheapest possible electrons on board.
  • Weekly, if you have an LFP battery. As above, this keeps the range estimate honest and is what the manufacturer asks for.
  • Occasionally, for cell balancing. Many cars do their fine balancing of individual cell groups near the top of the charge window. An occasional 100% charge, even on a nickel-based pack, helps the pack stay even. Once a month or so is plenty if your daily routine never goes above 80%.
  • In deep winter, before a cold-weather run. Cold reduces usable range and blunts regenerative braking anyway, so the regen argument against 100% matters less, and the extra buffer is welcome. There is more on this in our guide to EV charging in winter.

The one habit to avoid in all chemistries is charging to 100% and then leaving the car parked for days or weeks, especially somewhere warm. If you are going away and leaving the car at home, aim to park it at around 50 to 60% instead.

sections.h2: Set it once and stop thinking about it
sections.html:
Every modern EV lets you fix this in software in a couple of minutes, and the same screen is usually where the money savings live too.

  • Set the charge limit in the car's charging menu or the manufacturer app: 80% daily for nickel-based packs, 100% for LFP. Many cars let you save a second profile, so a long-trip 100% setting is one tap away.
  • Set a charging schedule so the car only draws power during your cheap window. On an EV tariff at 7 to 9p per kWh, against the 26.11p standard rate, the timing of the charge matters far more to your wallet than the level you charge to. Our guide to the best time to charge an electric car walks through the setup.
  • Pick the right tariff to put under that schedule. Dedicated EV tariffs, smart tariffs that control the charger for you, and standard off-peak deals all work differently; EV electricity tariffs explained compares the types and how to match one to your mileage.
  • Leave the car plugged in when it is home. Plugged in is not the same as charging: once the limit is reached the charger stops, and the car can use mains power rather than battery power for housekeeping like battery conditioning and app wake-ups.

Done once, the whole thing is automatic. The car charges to the right level, in the cheap hours, every night, and you only override it the evening before a big drive.

Not as a daily habit if your car has a nickel-based (NMC or NCA) battery: set 80%, enjoy slightly stronger regen and a calmer battery, and charge to full the night before long trips. Yes, happily and at least weekly, if your car has an LFP battery, because the manufacturer wants the calibration. And on public rapid chargers, stop at 80% regardless of chemistry, because the last 20% is the slowest and, at around 79p per kWh, the most expensive electricity you can put in the car.

Keep a sense of proportion, though. Average degradation across large EV fleets runs at roughly 2% of capacity a year, batteries carry warranties of typically 8 years or 100,000 miles, and the gap between good and careless charging habits is a nudge to that curve, not a cliff. The settings above are worth two minutes of your time; anxiety about the occasional full charge is not.

faq.q: Does charging my EV to 100% once damage the battery?
faq.a: No. A single full charge, or even a regular full charge before long trips, is normal use that every EV is designed and warrantied for. The thing that measurably accelerates battery ageing is leaving the car parked at or near 100% for long periods, especially in warm weather. Charge to full, drive off within a few hours, and there is nothing to worry about.

faq.q: How do I know if my EV has an LFP battery?
faq.a: Check the specification sheet or handbook, where LFP (lithium iron phosphate) is normally stated, or look at the charging guidance in the car's own screen or app. If the manufacturer asks you to charge to 100% at least weekly, you almost certainly have an LFP pack. Standard-range versions of a model are the most likely candidates, but always verify for your specific car.

faq.q: Is it bad to leave my EV plugged in overnight?
faq.a: No. Once the battery reaches the charge limit you have set, charging stops; the car is not trickling energy into a full battery all night. Manufacturers generally recommend leaving the car plugged in when parked at home, because the car can run battery conditioning and other background tasks from the mains instead of draining the pack.

faq.q: What charge level should I leave the car at if I am away for a few weeks?
faq.a: Around 50 to 60% is the sweet spot for storage in every chemistry. Avoid leaving it near 100% or letting it sit close to empty. If the car will be parked somewhere with power, leaving it plugged in with a modest charge limit set is ideal, as the car can maintain itself without running the pack down.

faq.q: Is the last 20% of charge more expensive?
faq.a: Not per kWh at home; you pay the same rate for every unit. On public rapid chargers, though, charging slows sharply above about 80% to protect the battery, so the last 20% costs you significant time at the highest per-kWh prices on the network, around 79p per kWh on rapids as of mid 2026. On road trips it is usually quicker and cheaper to unplug at 80% and stop again sooner.
